‘The All-treland football final for 1914
will remains an undecided issue, Kerry
were lucky to equalise the score in the last
two minutes of Sunday's contest at Croke
Park, Dublin. The laurels are, as yet,
“unclaimed.

Weslord Better

Wexford should have won on their
merits. Very few have ventured to dispute
it, ana these few must have had an oblique
viewiol the proceedings. It is jdle to.point
out that Kerty scored both against the
wind and with it, while Wexto’d only
scoted in one direction. Wexford were
‘much more frequently. attacking” against
the wind than with ity as the strain put
upon the Kerry ‘backs in the last half will
testity. Six wides for Wexford as agains

five tor Kerry in this moiety 1s also evi-
dence worth considering. ‘To two circum-
stances the Kerrymen owe their escape
from deteat—the temporary breakdown of
the Wexiord, back line in the fist few
minutes or the last balf and the souncness
of Lawlor and Mullins in thei own de-
fence:

A Feast of Thrilis

‘The game was 2 normally good one in
the first nalf, the standard of football being
high, and enjoyable to witness. Wexford
obtaliied two scores, both majors, and both
thoroughly deserved. Towards the interval
Kerry ootained 2 minor, and this success,

jst the wind, was bailed with satisface
tion by their supporters. Following the
resumption, the Wexford defenders ap-
peared to ‘have unaccountably collapsed,
aiid within (wo minutes there wasonly
point between the two teams. “The re-
covery of Wexford was quite as dramatic
as theic collapse, and theie forwards were
soon conducting a. fierce assault on the
opposite goal. Then began a struggic
which beat all records in point of intensity,
pace, science, and stamina. From both
wings (but particularly the left), and from
centrefeld the Wextocdmen fiercely assailed
the stronghold of their foes, and Kerry
‘were practically reduced to the delentives
Roniter, Reynolds, and Sean Kennedy
(Wexford) were superb, but so alo were
Lawlor and Mullins: (Kerry). Words tail

vietions, Mr. Walsh was wansferred, on
meagre notice, from a Post Office position
in Cork toa post in England. In conse-
quence of this banishment, Mr. Walsh
wrote resigning the presidency of the Cork
County Board. The Board refused to
accept his resignation, feeling that the
treatment meted out to Mr. Walsh was
both unjust and They
adopted a cesolution calling upon the Post-

this death-blow was dealt to his activities,

A meeting of the Leix and Ossory,County
‘Committee G.A.A. will be held in Mary-
boro’ on to-morrow (Sunday) November

‘On Sunday next our Junior burlers are
playing Kildare at Portleix for the final of
Leinster and whichever qualifies meets
Antrim in the All-Ireland tic. ‘The game
% Should be a great one as both teams are

tvenly matched, aad from what I know

Ee Kildare are leaving nothing to chance t0

win. Iwas greatly impressed by the dis-

play that some of our Juniors gave the

Giher bunday down in the Urbs Incacta by

the Suir! One player in particular, Quigley,

T think is his name, burled a great game.

Hiis striking was clever and he is a player

of very sound judgement. Others, too,

splayed a fine game and if they are not

4 already on our Junior team, they should be.

I hope to see a fine crowd at Portleix on

Sanday next to cheer our hopefuls on to
victory.

Councy Championships are over, and the

seurdy lads from Ballyroan have’ been de-

lared champions, all eyes are being turned

that is evideacia other counties, Or for that

matter what ic was’ some years ago in this

county... Well everyone is catitied 10 his

wa. opinion, but I muse aiffer from some

= of the arguments put forward, and I say as

to the progress of football in L:eix that it i

improving very much, and any person that

witnessed the recent final will bear me out,

Abiewasia fast gainc, and ut cimes the pace

“: was a cracker. Or course we have nvr

yet suanaged (o grasp fully che finer poins,

Of the game, but there is great promise

showa-amgngst. our foarballers, and espe-

tially amongst the Ballyroan boys. These

same boys sprung’a great surprise. on me

and a great many others, as well as on their

display givea in the recent final, and now

T would’ advise them to tackle at once the

formation of their county team, anu show

‘that they. mean to succeed where others

“have ‘failed.

The splendid spirit of brotherhood that
prevails among the Gaels could”have ao
stronger exemplification than that which
prevailed between these teams. A few
hours before this re-union they had fought
for the highest distinction of the Guelic
Athletic Association, with all the determina-
tion and well-trained energy at their coa
mand, but, as Mr, O'Reilly said in his

closing speech, they could battle as deter-

mined opponents for the decision of a great

issue, while they never forgot tne fact that

they were Irishmen all, aad) the provincial

ana county boundaries might have a special

geographical distinction, but Ireland was

sreater than any single county or aay single

province, and 1¢ was for Ireland as a whic,

that the Gaels of, Ireland stood, They

might be living in even more trying times
man the present, but he could say some-

ing of tne Gaels, and he knew that they
woud always be alive to the issues of their

day and the part tney would play if called
upon.

FIXTURES

‘Avia special meeting of the Central
Council held on Saturday the following
Groke Cup Championships fixtures. were
made :—

‘November 22nd, at Dublin (Croke Park)
—Cork v. Leitiim, football ; and Cork ¥.
Rotcommon, hurling.

December 13'h, at_Dungarvan—Hurl-
ing final, Kilkenny v. Cork or Roscommon.

The ‘All-Ireland Junior Championship
fixtures were made as follows :-

January 17th, at Limerick—Kerry vs
Mayo, football ; and Clare v. Galway,
hurling.

January 17th at Dublin—Cavan v.
Dablin, football 5 and Antrim v. Kildare or
Leix.

Mr. John Lalor, Kilkenny, reported that

he had bad to order J. Flannagan, Clare,
from the playing piteh on the occasion of
the hurling final. He recommended his
reinstatement, which was endorsed.

The expenses of Clare and Wexford
teams in inter-provincial matches were
adjusted passed, Resolution from
Cork County Board in reference to transfer
of Me. J. J. Walsh from Cork. was unani-
mously endorsed

‘The Croke Memorial conference
fixed for 1sth November, at Thurles

On the proposition of Mr. J. J. Hogan,
it was decided that provincial councils
thould conduct the initial rounds of the
Wolfe Tone Memorial Tournament. The
representatives of Connacht, Leinster, and
Munster agreed to this course. IC was
tlio decided that the provincial councils
should be held responsible for any loves
incurred, if their competitions proved un~
successful,

“The members present were— Alderman
Nowlan, presidents T. Kenny, J. J.
Hogan, J. O'Brien vice-presidents 5 Dan
Fraher and M. F, Crowe, trustees. Lein
